title: Recursion
content:
# DSA Interview Prep

## Q1: Why does Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm fail on graphs containing negative edge weights?

### Standard Answer
Dijkstra is a greedy algorithm based on the assumption that adding an edge to a path can only increase its total cost. Once a node is added to the visited set, its shortest path distance is finalized. If negative edges exist, this assumption fails; a longer path could reach a negative edge later and yield a lower total cost, but Dijkstra will not update the finalized node, producing incorrect results. Use Bellman-Ford instead.


## Q2: What is the spatial complexity bounds of Recursion?

### Standard Answer
The spatial complexity scales strictly as $O(1)$ auxiliary memory unless additional arrays or dynamic heap nodes are allocated, yielding $O(N)$ total space.
